# [The Tartan](https://the-tartan.org)

#### Regev wins 2025 Dickson Prize in Science

[Johnathan Subramanian](https://the-tartan.org/author/johnathan-subramanian/)

March 16, 2026

Loading…

* [Share using Native tools Share Copied to clipboard](https://the-tartan.org/2026/03/16/regev-wins-2025-dickson-prize-in-science/)
* [Share on Threads (Opens in new window) Threads](https://the-tartan.org/2026/03/16/regev-wins-2025-dickson-prize-in-science/?share=threads&nb=1)
* [Share on LinkedIn (Opens in new window) LinkedIn](https://the-tartan.org/2026/03/16/regev-wins-2025-dickson-prize-in-science/?share=linkedin&nb=1)
* [Share on Facebook (Opens in new window) Facebook](https://the-tartan.org/2026/03/16/regev-wins-2025-dickson-prize-in-science/?share=facebook&nb=1)

**Courtesy of Carnegie Mellon University** . Dr. Aviv Regev was awarded the 2025 Dickson Prize in Science on March 11.

On March 11, the 2025 Dickson Prize in Science was presented to Dr. Aviv Regev, a professor of biology at MIT and the head and executive vice president of Genentech Research and Early Development, for her trailblazing work in the field of computational biology. Dr. Regev is also the founder and co-chair of the international [Human Cell Atlas project](https://elifesciences.org/articles/27041) , which aims to identify all cell types in the human body to support developments in medicine, genomics, and beyond.

[Beginning in 1970](https://www.cmu.edu/dickson-prize/about-the-prize/index.html) , Carnegie Mellon has awarded the Dickson Prize in Science to the person identified to have made the most progress in the natural sciences, engineering, computer science, or mathematics in that year. Carnegie Mellon’s prize is awarded in conjunction with the Dickson Prize in Medicine from the University of Pittsburgh, which is awarded to the person who has made the most progress in medicine in that year. Both prizes are named after Dr. Joseph Z. Dickson and his wife, Agnes Fischer Dickson, who created a trust fund through their wills to fund the annual award.

In her acceptance speech, Dr. Regev discussed how the field of computational biology has changed over time, beginning with a discussion of how what began as a quality problem transformed into a quantity problem.

Regev noted, “Fifteen years ago, we would have focused on getting a few really exceptional high quality profiles of RNA.” However, she continued to explain that now, through new lab technology like droplet-based microfluidic devices, researchers are able to assess up to 55,000 cells at a time. “The measurements from every cell are going to be super noisy and super messy … But, the number of RNA profiles that we can sample is of many orders of magnitude,” she said.

Regev also discussed how producing these amounts of data can come with its own problems. “There’s no free lunch. You always have to pay. If we want to make sense of this noisy and messy data, we need AI algorithms,” she said.

By utilizing different AI algorithms, researchers are able to gain different insights from the same data set. “When algorithms cluster, we find cell types. When algorithms find continual trajectories or optimal transport maps, we see dynamical patterns, like cell differentiation, or tumorous cells. When the algorithm finds covariations across variables, we see gene profiles,” she explained.

By combining these findings and methods, scientists have produced over 200 million cell profiles through over 2,000 studies on samples from tens of thousands of individuals. These results have been used to develop the Human Cell Atlas.

Using these methods and past results, researchers have already been able to identify novel treatments and patterns in conditions like inflammatory bowel disorder (IBD), melanoma, and even COVID-19.

By using AI algorithms on the colon cell atlas, scientists were able to identify the cells which express the genes responsible for a portion of the irritability seen in IBD. The cancer cell atlas for melanoma was used to find the genes in some cancer cells that caused some patients to be resistant to existing treatments. Now, medical professionals are able to compare information on the cancer cells of some patients to the atlas to provide treatments designed to work best for them. For COVID-19, the Human Cell Atlas was used to identify 82 lung cells that likely expressed entry factors for the virus in February 2020. In March and April of 2020, an atlas made from cells of individuals who had survived COVID-19 was created and used to understand why the disease caused severe symptoms in some individuals but not others.

To conclude the awards presentation, Regev answered some questions from the audience moderated by Russel Schwartz, the head of Carnegie Mellon’s department of computational biology.

When asked about advice she had for students striving to work in computational biology in the future, Regev encouraged students to take advantage of their chance to enter an emerging field while new practices are continuously being developed and iterated upon.

“It’s an unbelievable privilege to be in that career stage when there aren’t textbooks and there isn’t a way things are done … Your ideas could be ‘the thing.’ No one knows what will be ‘the thing’ ahead of time,” she said.

She also noted that current students now have the opportunity to use AI to their advantage and that students should think creatively, saying “Think about new things in new ways. Instead of trying to retrofit a new thing into the old way of doing things say, ‘How do we liberate it? Maybe I shouldn’t try new sequences in my experiment in situ. Maybe I should think about running a lab autonomously.”
